points.text: Profile the endpoint to measure each call's latency and identify the longest-running step.
Combine independent sequential calls into a single batch query or use async parallelism.
Run EXPLAIN on the SQL, add indexes on foreign‑key columns used in joins.
Remove unnecessary tables from the join and select only required columns.
Rewrite complex joins using CTEs or subqueries to let the optimizer simplify execution.
Apply pagination or filter criteria to limit result set size.
Set a timeout budget based on the 95th‑percentile latency plus a safety margin (e.g., 2‑3×).
Implement retry with exponential backoff for transient DB timeouts.
Cache stable report data in memory or a materialized view to avoid repeated heavy queries.
Tune the DB connection pool size and per‑connection timeout to match the new budget.
Identify and isolate each external service call; replace sequential calls with parallel async calls where dependencies allow.
Wrap each service call in a timeout (e.g., 2‑3 s) and use a circuit‑breaker to fail fast if a downstream is unhealthy.
Profile the SQL with EXPLAIN ANALYZE; ensure join columns have appropriate indexes and avoid unnecessary tables.
Select only needed columns and apply filters before joins; consider materialized views or denormalization for heavy reporting.
Set the endpoint’s overall timeout budget to the SLA (e.g., 5 s) and allocate ~70 % to DB work, remaining to service calls.
Implement request‑level deadline propagation so downstream calls inherit the remaining time budget.
Cache stable report results or intermediate sub‑query results to reduce repeated heavy joins.
If joins remain costly, split the query into smaller batches and aggregate in application code.
Move sequential service calls into parallel async tasks to reduce overall latency.
Profile the query with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to identify slow joins and missing indexes.
Add covering indexes on foreign key columns used in join predicates and filter conditions.
Rewrite large joins using CTEs or subqueries to materialize filtered subsets before joining.
Replace SELECT * with only needed columns; add LIMIT/OFFSET if pagination possible.
Set PostgreSQL statement_timeout to a fraction of the endpoint SLA (e.g., 2‑3 s) and handle timeout exceptions.
Consider denormalizing frequently accessed aggregates into a materialized view refreshed periodically.
Split the reporting logic into separate, parallelizable queries instead of one massive join.
Add appropriate indexes on join and filter columns; verify using EXPLAIN ANALYZE.
Replace unnecessary columns with covering indexes to allow index‑only scans.
Materialize pre‑aggregated results in a summary table refreshed nightly or incrementally.
Set PostgreSQL's statement_timeout (or DB driver timeout) to a realistic budget, e.g., 5‑10 s.
Cache frequent report results in Redis or application layer to avoid repeated heavy queries.
Use pagination or LIMIT/OFFSET to fetch data in chunks, reducing memory and lock time.
Avoid SELECT *; fetch only needed columns to reduce I/O and CPU.
Split independent sequential calls into parallel async calls to reduce overall latency.
Cache immutable lookup data to avoid repeated DB hits in the reporting path.
Rewrite the SQL to select only needed columns and eliminate unnecessary table joins.
Add indexes on join and filter columns; verify with EXPLAIN that index scans are used.
Apply pagination or date range filters to limit rows processed by the query.
Set a timeout budget: e.g., 100 ms for DB, 30 ms for network, 20 ms for processing, total ≤150 ms.
Base timeout on p95 latency; choose timeout ≈ 2× p95 to cover tail without excessive wait.
Use Little’s Law (N = λ·W) to size thread pool: if target RPS λ=200 and avg latency W=0.15 s, need ≈30 workers.
Implement circuit breaker and exponential backoff for retries to avoid cascading timeouts.
